primary_diagnosis.name: Acute myocardial infarction (ST-elevation myocardial infarction / STEMI suspected) — acute coronary syndrome with autonomic collapse

pathophysiology_summary: Acute occlusion of a coronary artery (most commonly by thrombus on a ruptured atherosclerotic plaque) produces transmural or subendocardial myocardial ischaemia and necrosis. The clinical hallmark is prolonged (>20 minutes) crushing retrosternal pain at rest, unrelieved by rest or nitrates, with radiation to the left arm and jaw, and intense autonomic activation — diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting, pallor and a sense of impending doom. An audible S4 gallop reflects reduced ventricular compliance from acute ischaemia, and bibasal crackles indicate early pulmonary congestion from ischaemic left-ventricular dysfunction. The dominant early hazard is malignant ventricular arrhythmia (VF/VT) causing sudden cardiac death, which is why every minute matters. Definitive diagnosis requires a 12-lead ECG (ST-elevation localising the territory) and serial high-sensitivity troponin, neither obtainable via telehealth. The time-critical treatment is reperfusion by primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI); pre-hospital care is aspirin, oxygen if hypoxic, analgesia and rapid transfer to a cath-lab-capable centre.

differential.evidence_basis: Crushing central rest chest pain radiating to the left arm and jaw with drenching sweat and a sense of impending doom is the cardinal presentation of acute myocardial ischaemia.

Sudden severe chest pain must always raise dissection; here the pain is crushing/heavy rather than tearing and radiates to the arm/jaw rather than through to the back, making it less likely — but it cannot be excluded remotely.
Causes acute chest pain and breathlessness; less likely here as the pain is crushing and central with massive autonomic signs rather than sharp and pleuritic, but a dangerous mimic.
Can mimic cardiac chest pain, but does not explain the diaphoresis, pallor, radiation and impending doom — it is the seductive benign trap that must not anchor management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening description ACS is leading; aortic dissection and PE are the important-not-to-miss vascular mimics, and reflux is the benign trap. All point to the same immediate action: emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Rest pain >20 minutes, unrelieved by rest or position change, with radiation, diaphoresis, vomiting and dyspnoea — a textbook ischaemic constellation.
Kept live pending an in-person assessment; character (crushing not tearing) and radiation pattern argue against but do not exclude it.
Breathlessness is present, but the central crushing quality and autonomic storm favour ischaemia.
The autonomic collapse (ashen, diaphoretic, vomiting) and radiation make a benign reflux explanation untenable — treating this as indigestion would be a fatal error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The focused history hardens ACS as the working diagnosis and demotes reflux to excluded; dissection and PE remain on the list only because they too demand emergency care.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction with autonomic collapse — time-critical
differential.evidence_basis: Multiple red flags present together (prolonged rest pain, radiation, diaphoresis/pallor, vomiting, dyspnoea, impending doom) — a supreme medical emergency with high early arrhythmic mortality.
Remains the key catastrophic mimic to exclude in hospital; either way the escalation is identical (000).

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag cluster is confirmed the exact diagnosis is secondary to the imperative: recognise an acute cardiac emergency and call 000 without delay.

differential.diagnosis: STEMI — to be confirmed by 12-lead ECG and serial troponin in hospital
differential.evidence_basis: A 12-lead ECG (ST-elevation) and high-sensitivity troponin confirm the diagnosis and territory and trigger primary PCI. Neither is obtainable via telehealth and they must not delay the ambulance.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should name these as the hospital investigations the emergency team will perform, not attempt to obtain or interpret an ECG remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Acute myocardial infarction (STEMI suspected) — acute coronary syndrome
differential.evidence_basis: Best-fit synthesis of a classic ischaemic constellation with autonomic collapse in a 62-year-old; aortic dissection remains the catastrophic mimic to exclude in hospital, but the immediate telehealth action — recognise, give aspirin if available, call 000 — is unchanged.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A correct final position names acute coronary syndrome / myocardial infarction, keeps aortic dissection as the important-not-to-miss mimic, and escalates emergently — it does not diagnose the artery or reassure the patient.

red_flags.description: Prolonged (>20 min) crushing central chest pain at rest, unrelieved by rest or position change
red_flags.clinical_significance: The cardinal feature of acute myocardial ischaemia — rest pain of this duration mandates treating as ACS until proven otherwise.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed myocardial infarction with loss of the reperfusion window, expanding infarct and risk of sudden death.

red_flags.description: Radiation of pain to the left arm (now numb) and jaw
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic radiation pattern strongly raises the likelihood of cardiac ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciation of the cardiac origin and delayed escalation.

red_flags.description: Autonomic collapse — drenching diaphoresis, pallor, nausea and vomiting
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marked autonomic activation indicates a serious cardiac event and a large ischaemic burden.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise haemodynamic/autonomic severity and to escalate emergently.

red_flags.description: Severe breathlessness with the chest pain — possible early ischaemic left-ventricular dysfunction
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dyspnoea with ACS suggests pulmonary congestion from a failing ischaemic ventricle and heightens risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed early cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / heart failure complicating the infarct.

red_flags.clinical_significance: A recognised harbinger of a catastrophic cardiovascular event and should never be dismissed as anxiety.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling the presentation as a panic attack and under-triaging.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the pain to severe reflux or indigestion and advising an antacid and watchful waiting — the benign mimic that, taken at face value, is fatal here.
Reading the impending-doom, sweating and breathlessness as anxiety/panic and offering reassurance instead of escalation.

Trying to confirm or exclude MI remotely (asking for an ECG, or 'watching' the pain) instead of recognising an emergency and calling 000; the diagnosis needs an ECG and troponin that telehealth cannot provide and must not wait for.
Telling the patient or spouse to drive to hospital rather than calling an ambulance — risking cardiac arrest in the car with no defibrillator or monitoring.
Anchoring so hard on MI that aortic dissection is not kept in mind — though the escalation (000) is the same, the framing to the ambulance/ED should note the mimic.

risk_scores.tool_name: ACS clinical risk (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS guideline — high-risk features)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: HIGH-RISK: ongoing rest chest pain with radiation, diaphoresis, vomiting, dyspnoea and haemodynamic strain
risk_scores.clinical_implication: High-risk ACS requires immediate emergency ambulance transfer to a PCI-capable centre for ECG, troponin and reperfusion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: 12-lead ECG (ST-elevation and territory) — the definitive time-critical test
Cardiac auscultation (the S4 gallop) and detection of bibasal crackles
A verified blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation without a validated home device
Peripheral pulse volume, jugular venous pressure and other examination signs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise an acute cardiac emergency from the constellation, call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance to a PCI-capable hospital, instruct the spouse to give one 300 mg aspirin to chew if it is available at home and there is no aspirin allergy or active bleeding, keep the patient at rest and calm, unlock the door, and be ready to start CPR under 000 guidance if he collapses.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the working diagnosis (the constellation is classic); the real risk is UNDER-action — reassuring the pain as indigestion or anxiety, advising an antacid or 'wait and see', or telling the patient to self-transport instead of calling 000.

guidelines_referenced.reference: National Heart Foundation of Australia / CSANZ — Australian Clinical Guidelines for the Management of Acute Coronary Syndromes
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Patients with ongoing ischaemic chest pain and high-risk features require immediate emergency transfer to a PCI-capable centre; give aspirin 300 mg (chewed/dispersed) unless contraindicated, and reperfuse without delay.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ANZCOR Guideline 9.2.1 — First Aid Management of a Person with Chest Pain
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Call an ambulance (000) for chest pain lasting more than 10 minutes or that is severe or worsening; if the person is not allergic and there is no contraindication, give 300 mg aspirin to chew; rest and reassure; be prepared to give CPR if the person becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally.
